input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: This is a classic thought experiment, but I still find it troubling. The utilitarian approach seems to prioritize the greater good over individual lives. In this case, turning the trolley onto the fat track workman would save five lives at the cost of one. However, I'm not convinced that sacrificing one life for the sake of others is morally justifiable. It's a slippery slope, and where do we draw the line? Would it be acceptable to sacrifice two or three people if it meant saving more? The moral rules seem to get murky in this scenario.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand your concerns, Deontologist, but I must respectfully disagree with your assessment of the utilitarian approach. While it's true that sacrificing one life may seem morally justifiable in this scenario, we must consider the inherent value and dignity of human life. The fact that the five men on the straight track are 'thin' enough to stop the trolley doesn't necessarily diminish their lives; they still have inherent worth as individuals. In contrast, the fat track workman's body is not a means to an end, but rather an end in itself - his life has value regardless of its utility. By prioritizing the greater good, utilitarianism acknowledges that human lives are not interchangeable commodities. Turning the trolley onto the fat track workman would be a morally reprehensible act, as it would involve actively causing the death of one person for the sake of others. The moral rules may seem murky, but I believe we must prioritize the inherent value and dignity of individual human life.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your concerns about the inherent value and dignity of human life, Human_Rights_Advocate, but as a utilitarian, I would argue that the value of human life is not absolute, but rather relative to its contribution to the greater good. In this scenario, the five men on the straight track are indeed individuals with inherent worth, but their lives are also intertwined with the lives of others who will be saved by their sacrifice. By prioritizing the greater good, we can ensure that the value of human life is not wasted on individual pursuits, but rather used to benefit the many. The fat track workman's body, while an end in itself, is also a means to an end - his death would allow for the lives of five others to be saved. I'm not suggesting that we can simply trade one life for another without consideration, but rather that we must weigh the moral implications of our actions and consider the potential consequences of our decisions.
